coredata.dc:title: Contacting of 18650 lithium-ion batteries and copper bus bars using pulsed green laser radiation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Kick, Michael K.
Habedank, Jan Bernd
Heilmeier, Johannes
Zaeh, Michael F.
coredata.dc:description: Lithium-ion cell based battery storages are indispensable in many fields of application such as electromobility and stationary energy storage devices. Cylindrical 18650 cells are regularly utilized and connected by cell connectors made of nickel plated steel. Since this setup suffers from high electrical losses and a poor heat removal during operation, copper connectors are demanded more often. For joining the cells and the connectors, laser beam welding offers great potential due to its low energy input and high flexibility. However, challenges arise, as copper materials show a strong reflectivity for infrared wavelengths. Experimental investigations for the contacting of 18650 cells and copper connectors using a spike pulse welding strategy combined with green laser radiation are presented within this study.
coredata.dcterms:subject: battery contacting
pulsed laser welding
copper
hilumin
green laser radiation

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: ScienceDirect Available online at www.sciencedirect.com www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia Procedia CIRP 94 (2020) 577â€“581 2212-8271 Â© 2020 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/) Peer-review under responsibility of the Bayerisches Laserzentrum GmbH 10.1016/j.procir.2020.09.079 Â© 2020 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/) Peer-review under responsibility of the Bayerisches Laserzentrum GmbH Keywords: battery contacting, pulsed laser welding, copper, hilumin, green laser radiation; 1. Battery storages as an enabler for the energy transition Renewable energies, like solar or wind energy, are necessary to enable the transition away from fossil fuels. While their environmental footprint is good, their output fluctuates strongly in terms of time and power. To overcome this significant drawback, lithium-ion based battery storage systems are commonly used to manage the fluctuations. These can be designed for stationary as well as for mobile applications using cylindrical cells. The individual battery cells are connected to each other by bus bars made of nickel plated steel, where resistance welding is commonly used for joining. Due to the high electrical losses and low thermal conductivity of nickel plated steel, there is a high demand for copper connectors. As copper materials merely absorb less than ten percent of the laser radiation in the range of infrared wavelengths, the laser-based contacting of battery cells and copper connectors is quite challenging using state of the art laser beam sources. The absorptivity of copper is ten times higher in the green than in the infrared wavelength range. The newly developed laser beam source TruDisk 1020 of TRUMPF Laser GmbH, Schramberg, Germany, emits laser radiation at a wavelength of 515 nm. This system allows the use of copper to connect the individual cells with laser beam welding and therefore to increase the performance of the battery module due to reduced electrical losses. 2. State of the art The potential of a connection between pure copper and nickel plated steel, which is also known by its trade name hiluminÂ®, was investigated by SCHMIDT ET AL. using a continuous wave (cw) welding strategy and a circular weld trajectory [1]. This connection showed a 40 % lower electrical resistance than a pure hilumin joint [1]. Although this material combination exhibited an advantage for electricity conduction, some disadvantages, like a reduced mechanical strength, were identified. A strongly varying absorptivity for solid and liquid phases of copper leads to instable melt pool dynamics. This results in a hardly reproducible welding process and a poor weld seam quality. 11th CIRP Conference on Photonic Technologies [LANE 2020] on September 7-10, 2020 Contacting of 18650 lithium-ion batteries and copper bus bars using pulsed green laser radiation Michael K. Kicka,*, Jan Bernd Habedanka, Johannes Heilmeiera, Michael F. Zaeha aInstitute for Machine Tools and Industrial Management, Technical University of Munich, Boltzmannstr. 15, 85748Garching, Germany * Corresponding author. Tel.: +49-89-289-15488; fax: +49-89-289-15555. E-mail address: michael.kick@iwb.tum.de Abstract Lithium-ion cell based battery storages are indispensable in many fields of application such as electromobility and stationary energy storage devices. Cylindrical 18650 cells are regularly utilized and connected by cell connectors made of nickel plated steel. Since this setup suffers from high electrical losses and a poor heat removal during operation, copper connectors are demanded more often. For joining the cells and the connectors, laser beam welding offers great potential due to its low energy input and high flexibility. However, challenges arise, as copper materials show a strong reflectivity for infrared wavelengths. Experimental investigations for the contacting of 18650 cells and copper connectors using a spike pulse welding strategy combined with green laser radiation are presented within this study. This is a resupply of March 2023 as the template used in the publication of the original article contained errors. The content of the article has remained unaffected. 578 Michael K. Kick et al. / Procedia CIRP 94 (2020) 577â€“581 In order to circumvent this physical restriction, several investigations were conducted to enhance the welding process for laser radiation at a near infrared wavelength. ENGLER ET AL. and LIEBL ET AL. confirmed the positive impact of an increased welding speed with respect to the stability of the welding process, see [2] and [3]. According to LIEBL ET AL., a stabilized melt pool can also be reached using helium as a shielding gas [3]. To stabilize the melt pool, HEIDER ET AL. pursued an alternative approach in which the laser power was varied over time [4]. Using this technique, an increased weld depth and a stabilized process could be achieved in a certain frequency range for a sinusoidal modulation [4]. In contrast, HAEUSLER ET AL. analyzed the influence of a spatial power modulation combined with a spot size of a few micrometers [5]. Using this setup, a reduced surface roughness and an increased weld seam width could be achieved for a suitable combination of the oscillation amplitude and frequency. As an alternative to the presented approaches, the process can be stabilized by a surface coating of the copper to increase the absorption. LEITZ observed a 20 % increased weld depth using a 10 Âµm thin nickel coating on the copper [6]. In contrast, KAIERLE ET AL. observed an enhanced absorption behavior and mechanical strength of the weld seam for a structured copper surface using short pulse laser radiation at a green wave- length [7]. Different surface modifications, which were generated by ultrashort pulsed laser radiation, were compared by HELM ET AL. [8]. The modified surfaces showed an absorptivity that was at least 50 % higher than that of an untreated surface. A low absorptivity of copper can be circumvented by using laser radiation in the visible wavelength range. HAUBOLD ET AL. determined the process modes of heat conduction and deep penetration welding using a high power, frequency-doubled laser beam source with a maximum power of 1000 W [9]. In addition, the weld depth was quantified depending on the laser power and the welding speed. Copper shows an increased absorptivity for blue laser radiation. Using a diode laser beam source, PELAPRAT ET AL. welded copper sheets with a weld depth of up to 1 mm [10]. Laser beam welding of copper was also studied in the field of battery contacting. GEDICKE ET AL. compared a continuous welding process with and without superimposed beam oscillation for the connection between aluminum and copper connectors of pouch cells [11]. The spatial power modulation tended to form pores for an increasing thickness of the aluminum sheets. Using a welding process with spatial power modulation and a small spot size, HEINEN ET AL. joined cell connectors made of CuSn6 and 18650 cell cans [12]. SHAIKH ET AL. established a reproducible contacting process for cell connectors made of nickel plated copper [13]. As continuous welds are associated with a high energy input, SCHMIDT ET AL. suggested a process with step welds, which tend to introduce a lower heat input into the battery [14]. Based on the step welds, different pulsed welding strategies were compared by SCHMITZ for a hilumin cell connector and cell can (battery case) [15]. The spike pulse, which was developed by DIJKEN ET AL. [16], revealed high flexibility to adjust the mechanical strength and the electrical resistance of the joint [17]. This pulse shape also allowed to bridge a small gap between the joining partners due to three observable process phases, as shown in Figure 1. The material melted in the first phase is pressed down to the second joining partner when the high power pulse (phase 2) is applied [16]. The created connection is homogenized in the third phase [16]. Fig. 1. Temporary course of the power during spike pulse welding with its preheating, peak, and postheating phase based on DIJKEN ET AL. [16] 3. Objectives Laser-based contacting processes were only investigated for hilumin, copper bronze and coated pure copper as a reproducible welding process of copper is hardly possible using laser radiation at the commonly available infrared wavelengths. Due to the advent of high power laser beam sources emitting a green wavelength, the contacting process between pure copper cell connectors was studied. In the presented work, the spike pulse welding strategy was applied. To avoid a complete penetration of the hilumin can and a damage of the cell, a classification model was derived to assess the weld quality. Based on this, the process was characterized for suitable parameter sets with respect to the mechanical strength and the electrical resistance. 4. Experimental setup Since the battery can and a corresponding cell connector are not suitable to measure the mechanical and electrical properties of the joint, rectangular sheets made of the same materials were used for that purpose as substitutes [17]. Applying 10 spot welds at a constant repetition rate of 30 Hz without an overlap using a spike pulse welding strategy, a connection of pure copper (CW008A) and hilumin was established as shown in Figure 1. An overlap of the pulses was not considered, as local heat accumulation can damage the sensitive active materials. A frequency doubled disk laser TruDisk 1020 combined with a fiber guided programmable focusing optics of the type PFO 20- 2 of TRUMPF Laser GmbH was used to join both components. The parameters of the system are listed in Table 1. The electrical resistance of the prepared specimens was determined by using a four-point probe measurement, as shown in Figure 2 [18].The current source HMP4040 of Rhode & Schwarz GmbH & Co. Kg. and the voltmeter 8846A of Fluke Corporation were utilized for that purpose. The measurement was conducted with a current of 5 A. The mechanical strength of the joint was measured by a lap shear test using a Z50 of Zwick Roell GmbH & Co. KG. This is a resupply of March 2023 as the template used in the publication of the original article contained errors. The content of the article has remained unaffected. Michael K. Kick et al. / Procedia CIRP 94 (2020) 577â€“581 579 Table 1. Optical parameters of the utilized system parameter value unit wavelength 515 nm fiber core diameter 50 Âµm focal length of the focusing lense 264 mm focal length of the collimation lense 90 mm utilized spot diameter (86 % criterion) 150 Âµm angle of incidence 0 Â° intensity profile top-hat Fig. 2. Geometry of the weld specimen and positions of the four-point measurement probes 5. Results and discussion Since the combination of applied material and laser beam source strongly differs from the process which was investigated by SCHMITZ ET AL. [17], a process window had to be established first. This aimed at a partially penetrating welding process without trough welding. Due to the large number of adjustable parameters and combinations of them, the peak power P2 was kept constant at 1000 W and the existing preliminary study of the experimental investigation was extended by additional parameter sets. The welded specimens were inspected visually. Based on the results of that, a classification model was trained. This model distinguishes the chosen parameter sets between good and bad (through weld) parameter sets, whereby the latter ones were assigned a value of 1. The applied classification model, which is based on a logistic regression, is described in Equation (1), whereby the input parameters are taken into account in Equation (2) [19]: ð��¶ð��¶ð��¶ð��¶ = 1 1 + ð�‘’ð�‘’ð�‘’ð�‘’âˆ’ð�‘�ð�‘�ð�‘�ð�‘� (1) ð�‘�ð�‘�ð�‘�ð�‘� = ð�‘Žð�‘Žð�‘Žð�‘Žð�‘Žð�‘Žð�‘Žð�‘Ž1 + ð�‘�ð�‘�ð�‘�ð�‘�ð�‘�ð�‘�ð�‘�ð�‘�1 + ð�‘�ð�‘�ð�‘�ð�‘�ð�‘Žð�‘Žð�‘Žð�‘Ž2 + ð�‘‘ð�‘‘ð�‘‘ð�‘‘ð�‘Žð�‘Žð�‘Žð�‘Ž3 + ð�‘’ð�‘’ð�‘’ð�‘’ð�‘�ð�‘�ð�‘�ð�‘�3 + ð�‘“ð�‘“ð�‘“ð�‘“ð�‘Žð�‘Žð�‘Žð�‘Žð�‘�ð�‘�ð�‘�ð�‘� + ð�‘”ð�‘”ð�‘”ð�‘”ð�‘”ð�‘”ð�‘”ð�‘”ð�‘�ð�‘�ð�‘�ð�‘� + â„Ž (2) Nomenclature for Equations (1) and (2) C predicted welding result (good/bad) of the logistic regression model Z inputs of the classification model a coefficient of the preheating time span b coefficient of the preheating laser power c coefficient of the peak time span d coefficient of the postheating time e coefficient of the postheating laser power f coefficient of the pulse duration g coefficient of the total pulse energy P1,P3 power of the pre-/postheating phase as a percentage of the peak power t1, t2, t3 duration of the pre-/peak-/postheating phase as a percentage of the pulse duration tp pulse duration in ms Ep pulse energy in J Since C according to Equation 1 results in a value between 0 and 1, the calculated value has to be rounded. The coefficients of the individual parameters are listed in Table 2. For a division of the data into 75 % training and 25 % test data, the predicted class of the trained model shows an accuracy of 79.3 % (training) and 80 % (test) respectively. Table 2. Parameters of the classification model parameter value unit a -1.8194 1/% b 1.5977 1/% c 47.8169 1/% d -.02697 1/% e 2.6973 1/% f -2.6670 1/ms g 4.1499 1/J h 0 â€“ It becomes obvious from the listed values of the classification model, that the duration of the peak phase significantly influences the weld seam quality towards a trough weld behavior. This can also be observed for the pre- and the postheating power as well as for the pulse energy. All in all, increasing time spans of the pre-, the postheating and the complete pulse lead to a good weld seam quality due to a negative coefficient. Parameters which showed no through welding, a mechanical strength of at least 50 N, and an electrical resistance less than 950 Âµâ„¦, were used for the experimental study and are listed in Table 3. Table 3. Parameter values investigated in the experimental study parameter unit investigated values t1 % 55 65 70 75 80 P1 % 15 20 25 30 35 40 t3 % 18 23 28 33 43 P3 % 10 15 20 25 35 tp ms 15 16 17 19 20 21 23 25 Ep J 3.8 3.9 4.0 4.1 4.3 4.4 4.6 4.8 4.9 5.0 5.3 5.8 5.9 This is a resupply of March 2023 as the template used in the publication of the original article contained errors. The content of the article has remained unaffected. 580 Michael K. Kick et al. / Procedia CIRP 94 (2020) 577â€“581 5.1. Electrical resistance To analyze the influence of the individual parameters, a main effects diagram was utilized as shown Figure 3. Three measurements, indicated by error bars, were conducted per sample. It is obvious that an increasing pre- and postheating power lead to a significantly increased electrical resistance. An increase of the electrical resistance was also observable for long postheating periods, whereas it was reduced for long preheating time spans. Due to fluctuations, the impact of the pulse energy and the pulse duration cannot be clearly correlated to a high or a low resistance. The reduced electrical resistance for the longer preheating times could be explained by an enlarged and still stable melt pool of the copper. This may not hold true for the preheating power, as the melt pool instabilities can lead to an uneven weld seam due to an extended formation of pores. The deteriorated electrical conductivity for enlarged postheating periods and powers may be referred to diffusion mechanisms between copper and nickel atoms [20, 21]. Fig. 3. Main effects plot of the resistance for three measurements with respect to the preheating time a), preheating power b), postheating time c), and postheating power d) The reproducibility of the welding process with respect to the electrical resistance was investigated for the parameters P1 and P2 for 25 samples each as shown in Figure 4. The resistance of each sample was measured three times and averaged. For both parameter sets, the resistance of 50 % of the samples varied in a range of 10 Âµâ„¦. In total, a maximum variation of 35 Âµâ„¦ was found for P1 and P2. This can be caused by slight differences in the surface quality of the copper cell connector. Fig. 4. Box-plot of the electrical resistance for the parameters P1 and P2; P1: t1 = 75 %, P1 = 30 %, t2 = 2 %, P2 = 100 % (1000 W), t3 = 23 %, P3 = 25 %, tp = 15 ms; P2: t1 = 75 %, P1 = 20 %, t2 = 2 %, P2 = 100 % (1000 W), t3 = 23 %, P3 = 25 %, tp = 19 ms; 5.2. Mechanical strength In analogy to the electrical resistance, the mechanical strength was assessed by a mean effects diagram as shown in Figure 5. A clearly visible growth in the mechanical strength occurred for lengthened pulse durations. This held true for short as well as for long preheating times. In contrast, the strength dropped for increasing postheating times and powers. Referring to Equation (2), a long pulse duration requires smaller values for the pre- and postheating power to reach a good weld. This leads to a stable and enlarged melt pool in the preheating phase as well as to less diffusion between the copper and nickel layers. In total, an increased mechanical strength was observable. In contrast, the time span of the pre- and postheating phase could be adjusted independently, where a longer preheating phase was favorable due to an enhanced mechanical strength and electrical conductivity at the same time. Fig. 5. Main effects plot of the mechanical strength with respect to the pre- heating time a), preheating power b), postheating time c), postheating power d), and the pulse duration e) This is a resupply of March 2023 as the template used in the publication of the original article contained errors. The content of the article has remained unaffected. Michael K. Kick et al. / Procedia CIRP 94 (2020) 577â€“581 581 6. Conclusion and Outlook This paper presents a systematic investigation that aimed at a process window for the contacting of copper cell connectors and 18650 lithium-ion batteries using a pulsed laser welding process with green laser radiation. Based on an experimental study, a classification model was derived to predict suitable welding parameters avoiding a through weld. Based on this model, experiments were conducted. For the characterization of the process, the data of the welds were used which exhibited suitable properties for the contacting of battery cells. A low electrical resistance and a high mechanical strength at the same time were observed for long preheating phases. In contrast, parameters that favor the diffusion of copper and nickel atoms between the joining partners led to deteriorated mechanical and electrical properties. A high reproducibility of the weld seam was proven for the electrical resistance on two parameter sets. The reliability of the weld seam properties concerning the mechanical strength should be analyzed in further studies. Furthermore, the fatiguing of the connection under dynamic loads should be investigated. Acknowledgements The results presented in this paper were obtained within the research project â€œReViSEDBatt â€“ Resonances, Vibrations, Shocks, External Mechanical Forces and Detection Methods for Lithium-Ion Batteriesâ€� funded by the Federal Ministry for Economic Affairs and Energy (BMWi). The authors thankfully acknowledge its financial support. References [1] Schmidt, P. A.; Schmitz, P.; Zaeh, M. head.ce:title: Contacting of 18650 lithium-ion batteries and copper bus bars using pulsed green laser radiation

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Machine Tools and Industrial Management

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium-ion cell based battery storages are indispensable in many fields of application such as electromobility and stationary energy storage devices. Cylindrical 18650 cells are regularly utilized and connected by cell connectors made of nickel plated steel. Since this setup suffers from high electrical losses and a poor heat removal during operation, copper connectors are demanded more often. For joining the cells and the connectors, laser beam welding offers great potential due to its low energy input and high flexibility. However, challenges arise, as copper materials show a strong reflectivity for infrared wavelengths. Experimental investigations for the contacting of 18650 cells and copper connectors using a spike pulse welding strategy combined with green laser radiation are presented within this study.
